summarization.a: Scatter plot for the current density (J_SC, in mA cm^-2) showing the performance of the 10 nm and 20 nm MoO layers, and the three different plasma exposures, including their mean values with standard deviation.
summarization.b: Scatter plot for the open-circuit voltage (V_OC, in mV) showing the performance of the 10 nm and 20 nm MoO layers, and the three different plasma exposures, including their mean values with standard deviation.
summarization.c: Scatter plot for the fill factor (FF) showing the performance of the 10 nm and 20 nm MoO layers, and the three different plasma exposures, including their mean values with standard deviation.
summarization.d: Scatter plot for the power conversion efficiency (η) showing the performance of the 10 nm and 20 nm MoO layers, and the three different plasma exposures, including their mean values with standard deviation.

a.question: How do the 10 nm thick layers perform compared to the 20 nm thick layers?
a.answer: On average, the 10 nm thick layers have a higher current density and a higher open-circuit voltage. Consequently, the 10 nm thick layers also have a higher fill factor (FF) and a higher power conversion efficiency (η). This means that, on average, the 10 nm thick layers outperform the 20 nm thick layers.

b.question: Which MoOx process results in the highest open-circuit voltage (VOC) values?
What is the approximate upper VOC value reached by the best-performing 2-second O₂ plasma cells?

d.question: What was the best obtained power conversion efficiency (η)?
d.answer: About 10.5 %, which was obtained for 10 nm thick layer, which had a 2 second O2 plasma exposure.
